title: `podman run` has implicit limit on number of flags accepted
body:
### Issue Description

`podman run` fails at some point with a certain number of flags.

*Podman Version*

```
$ podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      5.2.2
API Version:  5.2.2
Go Version:   go1.22.9 (Red Hat 1.22.9-2.el9_5)
Built:        Fri Jan 24 12:31:47 2025
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

```
$ rpm -q podman
podman-5.2.2-13.el9_5.x86_64
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Attempt to start a container with something like the command I issue, with newlines for readability's sake:

```
podman run
            --name foo_bar_baz_e1_0
            --hostname node_foo.domain.bak
            --env-file /tmp/foo_bar_baz_e1_0.env
            --volumes-from retail-security-dev-1.8.2
            --privileged=False
            --read-only=False
            --memory 128GB
            --stop-timeout 90
            --restart=always
            --add-host node_foo:17.111.35.151
            --add-host node_foo.domain.bak:17.111.35.151
            --cap-drop SETGID
            --cap-drop SETUID
            --network slirp4netns:port_handler=slirp4netns
            --publish 7521:7520
            --ulimit nofile=102400:102400
            --ulimit core=0:0
            --ulimit memlock=32:32
            --ulimit stack=10240:10240
            --ulimit nproc=32768:32768
            --volume /path/to/mount/logs/batch/foo_bar_baz_e1:/usr/local/this-app/log
            --volume /path/to/mount/jks/this_app:/usr/local/this-app/jks
            --volume /path/to/mount/jks/other_app:/usr/local/this-app/other_app/jks
            --volume /path/to/mount/jks/common:/usr/local/this-app/common/jks
            --volume /path/to/mount/logback/batch/foo_bar_baz:/usr/local/this-app/logback
            --volume /path/to/mount/app-data/batch/foo_bar_baz:/usr/local/this-app/app-data
            --detach=True <docker_path>
```

2. Scratch your head and go, "What the hell?" 


### Describe the results you received

And here's the output of `podman logs <container>`, _ad nauseum, ad infinitum_:

```
{"msg":"exec container process `/bin/sh`: Argument list too long","level":"error","time":"2025-02-25T16:45:49.060398Z"}
{"msg":"exec container process `/bin/sh`: Argument list too long","level":"error","time":"2025-02-25T16:45:49.221699Z"}
{"msg":"exec container process `/bin/sh`: Argument list too long","level":"error","time":"2025-02-25T16:45:49.380698Z"}
{"msg":"exec container process `/bin/sh`: Argument list too long","level":"error","time":"2025-02-25T16:45:49.518972Z"}
{"msg":"exec container process `/bin/sh`: Argument list too long","level":"error","time":"2025-02-25T16:45:49.681946Z"}
{"msg":"exec container process `/bin/sh`: Argument list too long","level":"error","time":"2025-02-25T16:45:49.836146Z"}
...
```

### Describe the results you expected

Expected a healthy container with the settings from the flags.

### podman info output

```yaml
*Podman Info* - Specific keys available on request.
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

N/A

### Additional information

This happens every time it is attempted.

comments.body:
Please provide a proper reproducer that can actually be run by anyone. Your run flags depend on specific paths that work, but more importantly you do not share which container image is being used.
The error is from the oci runtime that tries to exec the container process, none of you podman run flags change the container process arguments in any way, so likely whatever image you are using defines such a long argument list that exceed the linux kernel limit thus the exec fails (check ARG_MAX).

Apart from that upstream only support the latest version so please test with the latest versions, if you use RHEL you should contact RHEL support instead.

> --ulimit memlock=32:32
>             --ulimit stack=10240:10240

these limits seem too low.

I think you are passing a too long environment variable (as I assume from `--env-file`).  That is counted against the `stack` limit in the kernel:

```
➜ podman run --memory 128GB --ulimit core=0:0 --ulimit memlock=32:32 --env FOO="$(seq 10000)" --ulimit stack=10240:10240 --rm -ti fedora /bin/sh
{"msg":"exec container process `/bin/sh`: Argument list too long","level":"error","time":"2025-03-06T09:58:40.132567Z"}
```
